results.possibleIdentity: James Floyd Scott (often known as Jim Scott) was an American physicist who worked at Bell Telephone Laboratories in the Quantum Electronics Research Department between 1966 and 1971. During this period, he was based at the Bell Labs facilities in Murray Hill and Holmdel, New Jersey. Scott is widely recognized for his pioneering work in ferroelectric materials and is often referred to as the "father of ferroelectric memory." He earned his B.A. in physics from Harvard University in 1963 and his Ph.D. from Ohio State University in 1966. His technical expertise in physics, specifically quantum electronics and lattice perturbations, led him to contribute to interdisciplinary projects. He is cited in historical reports of the era's art-technology collaborations, such as the Los Angeles County Museum of Art's (LACMA) Art and Technology Program (1967–1971), where he provided technical contributions to the work of artist R.B. Kitaj. This involvement aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a service organization that facilitated collaborations between Bell Labs scientists and the artistic community in the late 1960s. Following his tenure at Bell Labs, Scott held prestigious academic positions at the University of Colorado, various Australian universities, and the University of Cambridge.
